policy_statement: Promote second-hand initiatives and certify 5 second-hand stores each year.
mechanism_description: The city council commits to promoting and certifying second-hand initiatives to increase product reuse, lowering demand for new goods and the emissions from producing and distributing them.
primary_causal_pathway: Consumption patterns → ↓ demand for new product manufacturing → ↓ lifecycle (embedded) GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: City increases uptake and legitimacy of second-hand markets (reuse) via promotion/certification, shifting purchasing behavior from new to reused goods.  
2) Mediating process: Reduced throughput of virgin materials and industrial production lowers energy use and process emissions in extraction, manufacturing, and logistics; also reduces waste handling needs at end-of-life.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in economy-wide consumption-based (embedded) GHG emissions, primarily CO₂ (and some non-CO₂) associated with producing and distributing new goods.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding GHG emissions by reducing demand for new goods and the associated manufacturing and supply-chain emissions through increased reuse.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Increased reuse displaces new production, producing the largest climate effect through avoided upstream manufacturing/supply-chain emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While reuse can improve resource efficiency, the climate-relevant endpoint emphasized and typically largest is reduced GHG emissions from avoided production; it is not primarily about reducing hazard exposure (adaptation) or enhancing ecosystems (nature-based).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a program focused mainly on waste diversion volumes or landfill methane, it could resemble waste-sector mitigation; if framed strictly as “same service with fewer materials/energy,” it can look like resource efficiency. Under this mechanism, however, the defining effect is reduced consumption emissions from displaced new goods, which is mitigation.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that promotes/certifies second-hand reuse aims to increase reuse and thereby reduce demand for new goods; that consistently drives the same primary mitigation pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred: Specific instruments, targeted product categories (e.g., textiles vs electronics), and accounting approach (territorial vs consumption-based) would be handled at the individual policy level, not the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If second-hand promotion increases overall consumption (rebound) or involves long-distance transport that outweighs avoided production impacts for certain items, net mitigation may be smaller; classification remains Mitigation because the intended and typical dominant pathway is avoided new-production emissions.
key_indicators: “promoting second-hand initiatives”; “supporting access to second-hand products”; “certification”; “Certify 5 second-hand stores each year”; “sales route”
co_benefits: Waste reduction (less landfill/incineration); local economic development for reuse businesses; affordability/social inclusion (lower-cost goods)
instance_edge_case_notes: Certification could be construed as a mild regulatory/standards tool if tied to mandatory eligibility requirements, but the text frames it as promotional support rather than a binding mandate; no financial incentive is specified.
